summary:
The coronation of Otto I as Holy Roman Emperor (962 AD) by Pope John XII in Rome inaugurated the most powerful phase of the [[event:2187|Holy Roman Empire]] and established the pattern of German imperial politics that would shape European history for centuries. Otto, King of Germany since 936, had already demonstrated military genius by decisively defeating the Magyar raiders at the [[event:2938|Battle of Lechfeld]] (955 AD) — ending decades of Magyar devastation of western Europe — and had campaigned repeatedly into Italy to assert Frankish-German authority over the politically fragmented Italian peninsula.

Otto's imperial coronation revived the Carolingian ideal of a Christian Roman Empire but with critical structural differences: the German king, not the Frankish or Italian nobility, was now the primary power base, and the relationship between emperor and pope was from the beginning both cooperative and contested. Otto controlled papal elections and used churchmen as administrators, treating bishoprics and abbeys as instruments of royal governance — a system that worked brilliantly in the short term but planted the seeds of the Investiture Controversy that would tear apart emperor-papal relations in the 11th century. Otto's reign also saw the Christianization of northern and eastern Europe accelerated through the creation of new archbishoprics (Magdeburg, 968) designed to organize the church among newly converted Slavic and Scandinavian peoples. The Ottonian dynasty he established remained the most powerful secular rulers in Europe through the mid-11th century.
